symbols: oranges — the fruits of the earth, abundance of God's provision, and the transience of earthly sweetness that decays
peeled orange — the passage of time, the stripping away of external beauty, and the exposed interior of things
wine bottle — celebration, the fruit of the vine, and the fleeting pleasures of the world
sugar bowl — refinement and sweetness of earthly life, which passes away
wine glass — fragility and transparency, the brevity of life easily shattered
description: A tabletop arrangement on a dark surface features a white porcelain sugar bowl with two handles and a lid decorated with a floral motif, positioned at the left. A tall dark green wine or champagne bottle with a white label stands at center. A small green-tinted wine glass sits to its right. In the foreground lie two oranges -- one whole, one partially peeled with its rind curling outward. A colorful, ornately patterned textile or screen serves as the backdrop, featuring swirling floral and scroll motifs in warm reds, greens, golds, and blues.

scripture_refs.reason: 'There is nothing better for a person than to eat and drink and find enjoyment in their toil' -- the still life genre reflects Qoheleth's meditation on earthly pleasures under the sovereignty of God.
'Vanity of vanities, all is vanity' -- the vanitas tradition underlying all still life painting, where beautiful objects remind the viewer of transience.
'Wine that gladdens the heart of man... food that sustains his heart' -- the theological grounding of earthly provision as God's gift.
